content.description: Castles of Burgundy is set in medieval France. Everyone is building an estate by collecting and placing hex tiles. The goal: score the most victory points over 5 phases by completing regions on your estate.
There's a main board in the center — this is where tiles get drafted from. It has 6 numbered sections called 'depots' (numbered 1–6), each holding several hex tiles.
Each player also has their own estate board with colored hex spaces. Notice the numbers 1–6 on the estate too — these correspond to dice rolls, which is how tiles get placed.
Each color represents a type of tile:
Castles
Animals (pastures)
Ships
Buildings (8 different types)
Knowledge (special abilities)
Mines (income)
Tiles can only be placed on matching-color spaces on the estate board.
Each player starts with:
A personal estate board
2 colored dice
1 castle tile (already placed on the starred space)
1 Silverling (the game's currency)
3 worker tiles
The goods tiles for Phase A go face-up on the 5 goods spaces on the main board. Bonus tiles for each color go near the board.
Worker tiles adjust dice results. Each worker spent changes a die by +1 or −1.
They even wrap around — a 1 can become a 6, and vice versa.
Multiple workers can be spent on the same die. So if you really need a specific number, workers make it happen.
Each round is simple: everyone rolls their 2 dice at the same time. Then, in turn order, each player uses those 2 dice to take 2 actions.
Roll, then 2 actions — that's a round.
Each die can do one of these four things:
Take a tile — grab a tile from the depot matching your die number, put it in storage (you have 3 storage slots)
Place a tile — move a tile from storage onto your estate, on a space matching your die number
Sell goods — sell all goods of the type matching your die number for Silverlings and VP
Take 2 workers — use any die (number doesn't matter) to grab 2 workers
The first two — taking and placing tiles — are the heart of the game.
Say you roll a 3 and a 5:
Take a brown building tile from depot 3, put it in storage
Place a tile from storage onto space 5 on your estate (color must match, must be adjacent)
Two actions, one per die — that's a whole turn.
Say you roll a 1 and a 4:
Sell all goods matching depot 1 for Silverlings and VP
Take 2 workers (any die works, number doesn't matter)
Not every turn is about tiles — selling goods and stocking up on workers are smart plays too.
When a tile goes onto the estate, three things must be true:
The space number matches the die used
The space color matches the tile color
The space is next to a tile already on the estate (adjacency rule)
Remember, workers can adjust dice by ±1 to reach the number you need.
If a die can't be used for placing — or nothing looks appealing — use it for something else:
Take a new tile from a depot
Sell goods
Grab 2 workers
There's always something to do with a die. Workers help a lot too — spending 1–2 workers to adjust a die and make a placement happen is almost always worth it.
The big points come from completing regions — filling every space in a connected group of the same color.
Region size → Victory Points:
Timing bonus — complete regions early!
Finishing regions early is a big deal.

options.label: What about the color bonus tiles?

content.description: There's also a race: the first player to fill ALL spaces of one color across their entire estate claims the large bonus tile for that color. The second player gets the small bonus tile.
These are worth a handful of VP depending on player count — nice to grab but not worth chasing at the expense of region scoring.
Each tile color triggers a special effect when placed on the estate:
Castle: Immediately take a free bonus action — like having a third die of any value. Very powerful.
Ship: Collect all goods from any one depot. Also move up on the turn order track.
Animal: Score VP based on the animal shown. If the same animal type is already in that pasture, those score again too.
Mine: No immediate effect, but earns 1 Silverling at the end of every phase for the rest of the game.
Building: Each of the 8 building types has a unique effect.
Knowledge: Grants an ongoing ability or end-game scoring bonus.
Each does something different when placed:
Warehouse — immediately sell any one goods type
Carpenter — take any building tile from the main board
Church — take any mine, castle, or knowledge tile from the main board
Market — take any animal or ship tile from the main board
Boarding House — take 4 worker tiles
Bank — take 2 Silverlings
City Hall — immediately place another tile from storage (as if you had an extra die of any value)
Watchtower — immediately score 4 VP
One important rule: no duplicate building types in the same connected brown region.
There's a central black depot on the main board. Once per turn — in addition to the 2 dice actions — anyone can spend 2 Silverlings to buy any one tile from it. No die needed.
This is great for grabbing a specific tile you need without hoping for the right die roll.
That covers the essentials. A few things to keep in mind:
The game is 5 phases of 5 rounds each (25 rounds total). Depots refill with new tiles at the start of each phase.
At game end, leftover goods, Silverlings, and worker pairs are each worth 1 VP. Knowledge tiles may add end-game scoring.
Tip: try to complete small regions early — the phase bonus drops from 10 VP in Phase A to just 2 in Phase E.
